preFix.title: Applying a textured finish to a ceiling
preFix.subtitle: Create a stipple, comb, or swirl pattern on a bare or primed ceiling
preFix.excerpt: How to apply a ready-mixed textured ceiling compound to create a stipple, comb, or swirl pattern, including preparing the ceiling, loading and working the compound, forming a consistent pattern, and avoiding lap marks between wet and dry sections.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Artex Ltd product technical guidelines. City and Guilds Level 2 Painting and Decorating syllabus.

content.text: A
textured compound

applied to a ceiling hides surface imperfections in the plasterboard or plaster. It does not need the precision of a smooth skim coat. The challenge is consistency: apply the compound at a uniform thickness and keep the working edge wet so sections blend without lap marks. This is an intermediate job not because the technique is hard, but because working overhead for long periods while keeping the pattern consistent takes practice.
Step 1, check the ceiling

The ceiling must be clean, dry, and free of flaking paint or loose plaster. Any cracks must be filled with a flexible interior filler, allowed to cure, and sanded flush. If the ceiling has been painted with a gloss or semi-gloss paint, lightly sand the entire surface and apply a stabilising primer: the textured compound will not bond to a shiny surface. Mask the coving, walls, and light fittings with masking tape and drop sheets.
Step 2, prime the ceiling if bare

If the ceiling is bare plasterboard or fresh plaster, apply a mist coat of emulsion (1 part water to 4 parts paint) and let it dry fully before applying the compound. A raw surface draws moisture out of the compound too quickly. This shortens the
open time
and causes the compound to skin before the pattern is formed.
Step 3, divide the ceiling into manageable sections

Do not attempt to apply compound to the whole ceiling at once. Divide the room into sections of about 2 m × 2 m and work one section at a time, blending the edge of each into the next before it skins. For a small room, two halves is a workable division. For a large room, work in thirds with a helper if possible.
Step 4, apply the compound

Load the compound into the roller tray. Apply with the 9-inch roller, working in one direction across the section. The compound should be applied at an even thickness of approximately 3 to 4 mm: thick enough to hold a pattern, thin enough not to sag. Cut in around the edges with the 50 mm brush. Work quickly to keep the section uniformly wet.
Step 5, form the pattern
Immediately form the chosen pattern into the wet compound. For a
stipple pattern

: press a clean dry stipple roller or a sponge float into the compound and lift straight up, repeating across the section. For a comb pattern: drag a serrated comb tool through the compound in overlapping arcs. For a swirl: use a clean dry plastic float in circular sweeping motions. Whatever pattern is chosen, maintain the same pressure and rhythm throughout: variation in pressure creates variation in depth, which shows as irregular texture once painted.
Step 6, blend section edges

As soon as one section is patterned, move immediately to the adjacent section and apply compound overlapping the edge of the first section by 50 to 100 mm. Work the pattern into and through this overlap area while both sections are still wet. If the first section has already skinned, apply a small amount of compound at the join and blend: but the join will be slightly visible in certain light. Prevention is better: keep working without stopping.
Variations

Ready-mixed textured compounds can be tinted to a pale colour before application. This is useful where the ceiling will not be painted after texturing and the white finish is too stark. For a heavier texture, apply two coats. Pattern the second coat while the first is still slightly tacky but firm enough not to move. A skip-trowel or Santa Fe texture is made with a wide taping knife dragged randomly across the surface, leaving irregular flat patches among the texture.
Care

A textured ceiling can be painted over with emulsion once fully cured (24 to 48 hours). Use a thick-pile roller that reaches into the texture valleys. Two coats of emulsion are usually needed for even coverage. Spot repairs to damaged areas are possible by applying compound, patterning to match, and painting after curing: the match is rarely perfect in raking light, but functional.

attrs.fix: If the compound is still soft, feather the join with a damp sponge and reapply the pattern. If cured, apply a thin skim of fresh compound over the join, pattern, and allow to cure before painting.
attrs.cause: First section was allowed to skin before the adjacent section was applied and blended.
attrs.symptom: Visible line at the join between two sections

attrs.fix: Scrape off the sagging area immediately with the filling knife and reapply at a thinner layer. If the compound has already started to cure, allow it to harden, scrape back level, and apply again.
attrs.cause: Applied too thickly in one pass; or compound too wet.
attrs.symptom: Compound sags or drips off the ceiling

attrs.fix: No structural fix for an already-cured inconsistent pattern other than skim-coating the ceiling smooth and re-texturing. Prevention: work in manageable sections and stop to rest between sections if needed.
attrs.cause: Pressure or tool angle varied during application; worker fatigue overhead.
attrs.symptom: Pattern is inconsistent across the ceiling

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
